sections.title: Citizenship
sections.subtitle:
Econ/Civics
Ethics
Current Events
Community Read Alouds
sections.subject: Citizenship

entries.title: About the Course
entries.content:
This course includes the following topic(s): Econ/Civics: Grade 11 (Canada), Ethics: Part 5, Current Events: Grades 9-12, Community Read Alouds: Grades 1-12

About Econ/Civics: Grade 11 (Canada)
This course covers a variety of citizenship topics. Students spend the first term learning the art of asking questions as a means of relationship building. The second term focuses on government as students explore topics of government through a historical survey of Canada's Prime Ministers. The year finishes with a term of economics where students learn a variety of economic theories.

About Ethics: Part 5
Students will explore characteristics shared by all Image-Bearers and learn how to guard against good servants becoming bad masters by continuing Charlotte Mason's text written directly to students. Students finish Book II in this course.

About Current Events: Grades 9-12
Students are exposed to current events by reading from a variety of sources and having conversations with teachers. Topics such as scientific discoveries, the arts, and human interest stories are generally suitable.

About Community Read Alouds: Grades 1-12
This is meant to be a read-aloud for all ages to enjoy together in the afternoon at least 1 time a week. We encourage you to choose a book that is specific to your region. However, we will offer some suggestions in the Grades 1-3 Literature lessons and Grades 4+ Citizenship lessons. The suggested book titles will be available June 1st in the lesson plans.

entries.title: Placement & Combining Tips
entries.content:
Econ/Civics: Grade 11 (Canada)
Lessons are occasionally integrated with composition lessons.
The book The Prime Ministers: Canada's Leaders and the Nation They Shaped will be read over two years (Grades 10 and 11). However, since this is the first year this book will be used, Grades 10 and 11 will have the same readings in 2026-2027.

Ethics: Part 5
Ourselves by Charlotte Mason is scheduled over five years. In a multi-age context, students can jump into the book wherever the class is reading. Otherwise, students should begin the book in Part 1.

Community Read Alouds: Grades 1-12

entries.content:
Permission to print for non-commercial use. See Alveary group use policy to use lessons
in a group context.

LINKS: Click text or scan the QR code in the top corner of the lesson plan pages to view
online resources associated with the lessons.

Responsibility for previewing all links rests with the teacher. All links were checked at the
time of publication; however, websites change frequently and may contain objectionable
content. Please report broken links by contacting us through our website.

Current Events: Grades 9-12
☐ Set up a Citizenship Notebook. You can do it multiple ways, but having a 3-ring binder, lined notebook paper, and about 5 tabbed dividers often works well. You can read more about how to set one up and suggestions for various sections in the Quick Links. 

☐ Make a Calendar of Events for Current Events lessons by drawing a calendar grid in a notebook (or print a blank calendar from the internet).

books.title: The Art of Asking Better Questions
The Prime Ministers: Canada's Leaders and the Nation They Shaped

links.label: The World and Everything In It

blocks.text: If you, the student, are learning at home, work to schedule a time with your teacher when you can complete the lessons from Ourselves or your Ethics reading together, as these lessons are much richer when shared and discussed.
Connect back to the previous lesson. (Optional for Current Events).
For news sources: Scan the headlines first to get an overview, and then choose a story to read.

Read the selection assigned or the news story chosen for the time assigned. For rich, dense, or more challenging books such as Ourselves, it is a good idea to read them aloud. Keep an eye out for quotes to put in your commonplace book or events or characters to put down in your Book of Centuries.

Process and integrate your reading by narrating either orally or in writing. Some lesson plans will give you a suggestion of how best to narrate. To keep yourself on track, you might want to set a timer so you stop reading with enough time to narrate.

After your narration, think about how what you read connects to other subjects, other books, other things you've read or watched, or seen in the past or present. For current events, look up where events occurred on a map and ask questions together to discern the quality of the piece. (e.g., Are there sources cited? Is there an obvious bias? If so, what do other authors have to say on this subject? Are arguments supported by evidence? Is that evidence credible?) Consider reading an alternative perspective.
Record important ideas in your Citizenship Notebooks. Make a calendar of current events.

These lessons are written directly to students, but to assist them throughout the year, please read through this section and the lesson plans so you are familiar with the instructions and flow of the lessons. You may also want to preview and choose which news sites to offer students.

Those educating at home might pick one lesson to read aloud (such as the Ethics readings on Cultural Engagement, Conscience, etc.) with their students to keep up the habit of oral narration and foster conversation and relationship and then simply give students these lesson plans and the rest of the books and check in with them throughout the term for conversation, support, and accountability. 

It is valuable for those in a classroom setting to also have a mix of lessons, which students complete themselves silently, and others read aloud and narrated together. Classroom settings lend themselves well to additional discussion and sharing at the end of lessons, after everyone has narrated either together or independently in writing.

lessons.title: 20m Ethics: Part 5 - Lesson 1
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Ourselves: Book II

➜ INTRO
Divide readings into 11 sections evenly over the term; try to include whole stories or episodes if applicable so that you are not stopping in the middle of a section (around 3 pages a week). Watch for examples from your readings that reflect the moral virtues or vices that embody the sections read.

➜ READ & NARRATE
p.104-136, divided across the term

lessons.title: 40m Econ/Civics: Grade 11 (Canada) - Lesson 1
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: The Art of Asking Better Questions, Journal or Notebook, Bible

➜ READ
Intro-Ch.1, p.3-25

➜ REFLECT
Think about the individuals that you interact with on a daily basis. Identify someone that askes good questions and ask that person what motivstes him or her to ask good questions..

lessons.title: 20m Current Events: Grades 9-12 - Lesson 1
lessons.body:
➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS 
Choose an article to read from one of the recommended sites. Write a few bullet points about the event. Enter daily stories on your Calendar and locate unfamiliar places in your atlas.

lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: The Art of Asking Better Questions, Journal or Notebook, Bible

➜ READ
Ch. 2, p.26-39

➜ REVIEW & LIST
Review the italicized questions throughout this chapter and list three questions in your journal or notebookthat were intriguing to you. Explain why.

lessons.title: 40m Econ/Civics: Grade 11 (Canada) - Lesson 3
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: The Art of Asking Better Questions, Journal or Notebook, Bible

➜ READ
Ch. 3, p.40-57

➜ LISTEN & Track
Divide a piece of paper into four squares. Write one of the four levels of questions in each square. One day this week, listen for the four levels of questions being asked by those around you and track how many times you hear the four levels of questions throughout the day.

lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: The Art of Asking Better Questions, Journal or Notebook, Bible

➜ READ
Ch. 4, p.61-71

➜ INTERVIEW
Identify one adult and one person your age to interview using David Brooks' questions listed on p.69. Give yourself plenty of time to interview each person. The goal is to get to know the person better, not simply to get through the questions.

lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: The Art of Asking Better Questions, Journal or Notebook, Bible

➜ READ
Ch.5-6, p.72-92

➜ WRITE
In your own words, list and explain the six reasons Jesus asked questions.
lessons.teacherNotes: After you read ch.5-6, Review Resource 4: Fascinating Facts About The Questions Jesus Asked on p.181-185.

lessons.title: 40m Econ/Civics: Grade 11 (Canada) - Lesson 6
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: The Art of Asking Better Questions, Journal or Notebook, Bible

➜ READ
Ch.7, p.93-101

➜ REFLECT & ASK
Reflect on the questions you have regarding God, the Bible, and faith. Ask the members of your family what questions they have regarding God, the Bible, and faith. Identify the questions that you have in common and as a family, take steps towards answering those questions.

lessons.title: 40m Econ/Civics: Grade 11 (Canada) - Lesson 7
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: The Art of Asking Better Questions, Journal or Notebook, Bible

➜ READ
Ch.8-9, p.105-123

➜ INTERVIEW
Identify a person who is responsible for leading people and schedule a time to interview that person. Identify questions from ch. 8-9 (especially from p, 111 & 116) to ask during the interview. Reflect on your own leadership influence and ask yourself the questions on p. 111 & 116. If time permits, interview several leaders and compare their responses.

lessons.title: 40m Econ/Civics: Grade 11 (Canada) - Lesson 8
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: The Art of Asking Better Questions, Journal or Notebook, Bible

➜ READ
Ch.10, p.126-137

➜ PLAN & IMPLEMENT
Designate a journal to be your Questions Journal. Reflect on the nine practices from ch. 9 and organize the journal accordingly. Do not be tempted to rush this assignment. The more time you spend creating a Questions Journal, the great likelihood you will see a transformation in how you ask questions.

lessons.title: 40m Econ/Civics: Grade 11 (Canada) - Lesson 9
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: The Art of Asking Better Questions, Journal or Notebook, Bible

➜ READ
Ch.11, p. 138-150

➜ PRACTICE
Identify a family member or friend to participate in a question-asking exercise with you. On p.147, read #6 "Hold a questions-only conversation with others." Once you both understand the exercise, initiate the conversation and see how long the discussion lasts. Keep practicing until the exercise becomes more natural.

lessons.title: 40m Econ/Civics: Grade 11 (Canada) - Lesson 10
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: The Art of Asking Better Questions, Journal or Notebook, Bible

➜ READ
Ch.12-Conclusion, p.151-160

➜ RECEIVE FEEDBACK
Identify two people that you interact with consistently and ask them to provide feedback on how you ask questions. Use the questions on p.152-153 to guide the conversation. The purpose of this exercise is to develop the skill of asking better questions. Do not be offended by the feedback you recieve. Embrace the feedback and develop action steps that will help you further develop the ability to ask great questions.

➜ REFLECT & REVIEW

Exams

terms.content:
Current Events: Grades 9-12
· Write a journal entry reflecting on any world event from your news reading this term.

Ethics: Part 5
· Ourselves: Book II: What is the function of Conscience? OR Contrast Willfulness and Will, giving examples from your reading.

Econ/Civics: Grade 11 (Canada)
· Explain how curiosity aids in developing the skill of asking great questions.

· If a friend asked you, "Why is it important to develop the skill of asking better questions?" how would you reply?

Current Events: Grades 9-12
· Write a journal entry reflecting on any world event from your news reading this term.

Ethics: Part 5
· Ourselves: Book II: What do you have to say about "the way of the will"? Distinguish between the obedience of habit and that of choice, giving examples.

Current Events: Grades 9-12
· Write a journal entry reflecting on any world event from your news reading this term.

Ethics: Part 5
· Ourselves: Book II: Write on the capacities and disabilities of the soul OR Show how fit and necessary the knowledge of God is to man. Is it inevitable?
